updates.summary: Provident Realty proposed a seven-building, 102-acre data center campus on land owned by Worthington Steel. Over 100 people attended the Planning Commission meeting with 20 speaking against it. The developer announced it was 'slowing down' its approach and withdrew its proposal on Oct. 7, 2024, but did not end its pursuit. As of March 2025, no updated proposal had been submitted. The proposed buildings would be 120 feet tall, double the town's 60-foot height limit.

Peculiar's Planning Commission Board announced an ordinance on Oct. 3, 2024 to remove data centers from the city's zoning ordinance, effectively blocking Diode Ventures' $1.5B Harper Road Technology Park project. The project proposed up to seven data center buildings, each spanning 300,000 sq ft, near a new Evergy substation. Opposition group 'Don't Dump Data in Peculiar' raised concerns about visual impact, noise, and property values. Hundreds of residents in Peculiar and nearby Raymore called on leaders to reverse the zoning decision.

Oliver County adopted a 3-year moratorium on data centers and cryptocurrency mining on December 12, 2024, after over 200 people packed the Betty Hagel Memorial Civic Center in Center, ND. The Planning & Zoning Board passed the moratoriums unanimously, and the Commission voted 3-0 (Rob Schmidt, Dave Berger, Lee Husfloen) in favor. Residents cited noise, heat, space concerns and rising utility rates.

Tract received approval for a 708MW hyperscale data center consisting of 12 buildings up to 80 feet tall and 2.53 million sq ft, plus an electrical substation and several hundred generators with over a million gallons of stored fuel. The project would double the city's water consumption at up to 2.93 million gallons/day. Officials signed NDAs with Tract. Castle Rock Township sued for breach of the Orderly Annexation Agreement on Jan. 14, 2025. The Coalition for Responsible Data Center Development, a 501(c)(3)/(c)(4) non-profit founded by Kathy Johnson, organized opposition. Environmental Coalition of Farmington MN members packed council chambers. State legislators introduced a bill to regulate hyperscale data center zoning.
opposition_groups: Coalition for Responsible Data Center Development

updates.summary: Marshall County became the first Indiana county to pass a data center moratorium in early February 2025. Commissioner and zoning board member Stan Klotz pushed for the pause, citing concerns about developments in nearby areas like New Carlisle. Residents voiced concerns about water use, environmental impact, and effects on electricity bills. The moratorium was followed by White County (October) and Putnam County (November).

Amazon planned a multibillion-dollar data center near the Xcel Sherco coal-fired power plant in Becker, proposing 250 diesel backup generators. In March 2025, the Minnesota PUC ruled Amazon must obtain a Certificate of Need, placing the burden on Amazon to demonstrate necessity and evaluate sustainable alternatives. Environmental advocate Sarah Mooradian of CURE MN argued that even generators not connected to the grid have huge impacts. In May 2025, Amazon abandoned the project, citing uncertain development timelines for permitting. Minnesota also eliminated a sales tax exemption on data center electricity.

Thor Equities proposed a $1 billion data center on 810 acres (building on about 20%) near Milan. Despite the Planning Commission recommending denial, the Board of Trustees unanimously approved rezoning of 522 acres in July 2025. PACT (Protect Augusta Charter Township) gathered 957 signatures in two weeks (needed 561) to force a public referendum. The special election is expected May 2026 at earliest. PACT is confident residents will vote the project down. Opponents fear noise, light pollution, destruction of rural character, higher utility bills, and brownouts. Thor committed to abiding by the township's noise ordinance.

Oppidan halted its data center project citing lengthy permitting for diesel generators. MCEA (Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy) sued the city in August 2025, alleging officials hid information and approved a faulty AUAR (Alternative Urban Areawide Review) environmental study. MCEA argues the review failed to adequately analyze water withdrawals from the Mount Simon-Hinckley Aquifer that supplies drinking water to ~1 million Minnesotans. The proposed development could consume 500,000 to 30 million gallons of water/day. Even though Oppidan backed away, the lawsuit remains necessary because the approved AUAR could allow future data center proposals. Briefing expected April 2026.

Applied Digital signed a 15-year lease with CoreWeave for 250 MW of capacity at its Ellendale campus. Construction of the $7B AI data center is underway. Residents wearing 'No Eminent Domain' t-shirts packed city meetings; a sign read 'My land, my choice.' Resident Leon Westerhausen opposed the facility citing land development impacts. Retiree Renee Hutchinson wished she had known about it before moving to Ellendale. The facility was expected to be fully operational by October 2025.

Applied Digital's $3B, 280 MW Polaris Forge 2 data center broke ground on Sept. 15, 2025 southeast of Harwood. An annexation battle erupted between Fargo and Harwood; Fargo's City Commission considered annexing 800 acres of land. Harwood Mayor Blake Hankey told Fargo to 'back off' and said most residents support the center. Several college students spoke against it citing environmental concerns, increased crime, and effects of AI on students. Fargo withdrew its annexation attempt in December 2025.

Menomonie signed an NDA with Balloonist LLC in February 2024, over a year before announcing the $1.6B data center proposal in July 2025. Over the following six months, residents coordinated a local opposition campaign. Mayor Randy Knaack announced on Sept. 22 that the city was no longer interested in pursuing the project. On January 5, 2026, the City Council revised a zoning ordinance to bar Balloonist from moving ahead. The data center would have used approximately 75,000 gallons of water per day and brought 50-75 full-time jobs.

White County's Board of Commissioners passed a moratorium on data center development on October 20, 2025, after residents packed public meetings in opposition. The moratorium was prompted by a proposed project near Wolcott. Residents voiced concerns about water availability and process transparency. White County joined Marshall (February) and Putnam (November) as Indiana's three counties with data center moratoriums, which conflict with a state government actively courting tech projects through a 2019 law granting sizable tax credits for computer equipment purchases.

The Yorkville City Council unanimously approved Project Cardinal on Nov. 10, 2025 -- a 1,037-acre data center campus with 14 two-story warehouses, developed by Pioneer Development LLC. However, a resident filed a lawsuit arguing the project would cause sound pollution and decreased property values. An online petition opposing both Project Cardinal and a separate Project Steel in Yorkville gathered over 1,300 signatures. The lawsuit appeared headed for settlement after a Dec. 30 hearing. NOTE: This appears to be Yorkville, IL (Kendall County), not Yorkville, WI.

Lordstown was one of the first Ohio municipalities to institute a moratorium on data centers. In October 2025, developer Bristolville 25 Developer LLC submitted site plans for a 133-acre, $3.6B data center in Trumbull and Mahoning counties. The council passed a ban on data centers in November, but later rescinded it for a 180-day moratorium after legal advice. Bristolville 25 filed suit in the Ohio Supreme Court seeking a writ of mandamus to force officials to process the application. The court dismissed the case against the Village Solicitor on Dec. 24, 2025, but denied dismissal against the village. The developer filed 3,000+ pages of evidence in February 2026.
updates.company: Bristolville 25 Developer LLC / BHGH Properties LLC

updates.summary: Two data center developers are eyeing over 400 acres of undeveloped land in Foristell, a growing city along I-70 straddling St. Charles and Warren counties. A public hearing was delayed from Jan. 5 to Jan. 20, 2026 to find a larger venue. Resident Phyllis Elizabeth Wall brought a petition with 150+ signatures. The Change.org petition has 784 signatures. Residents cite concerns that Foristell lacks infrastructure and oversight for such development.

Putnam County passed a one-year moratorium on wind, solar, data centers, and small modular nuclear reactor projects in November 2025. The moratorium was intended to give leaders time to understand long-term impacts and update zoning rules. The third Indiana county (after Marshall and White) to enact a data center moratorium. The moratoriums conflict with state policy favoring tech projects and a 2019 law granting sizable tax credits for computer equipment purchases.

The proposed 1.8 million sq ft 'Project Loon' hyperscale data center was kept secret from the public until September 2025, despite city officials discussing initial details as early as September 2024. Hermantown changed land use plans and approved a preliminary environmental review before the public knew it was a data center. On Oct. 20, the City Council voted 4-0 to rezone 200+ acres, with nearly 300 attending and dozens testifying against. The 'Stop the Hermantown Data Center' Facebook group gained 3,500+ members. SHDC (a non-profit LLC) partnered with MCEA to appeal the city's environmental study. Residents launched lawn signs, canvassing, and door-knocking campaigns.

Township enacted a unanimous six-month moratorium on Nov. 20, 2025 after ~2 hours of public comment. Trustee Bob Wilson confirmed Meta was behind the project, estimating a $9B investment and the center occupying at least 20% of the 1,000-acre space. The group 'Stop the Data Centers Livingston County' said the moratorium as passed did not apply to the current developer. In December 2025, the developers withdrew the rezoning request, stating they wanted to 'honor the current moratorium.' FracTracker Alliance celebrated the withdrawal as a community win.

Residents in Fredonia and Saukville formed a joint coalition to oppose new power-line routes serving the Vantage Data Center planned for Port Washington. The 'Responsible Energy Alliance' (~500 members) and the 'Protect Fredonia Coalition' (chaired by Pastor Patti Plough) are fighting against American Transmission Company's proposed routes of 96-123 miles of new transmission lines and up to 5 new substations. ATC submitted its application to the Wisconsin Public Service Commission. The PSC will decide on routes in spring 2026.

RadiusDC is planning to build two 100,000 sq ft data centers in Plainfield using 24 MW of electricity and about 700 gallons of water/day. The Plainfield Plan Commission unanimously approved the primary plat petition on Jan. 5, 2026, after more than a dozen concerned residents opposed the project during public comment. A Change.org petition opposes the project citing lack of transparency.

Gemini Capital proposed a $1.9B, 500 MW hyperscale data center on 164 acres in eastern Sioux Falls. The City Council voted 7-0 on Jan. 6, 2026 to rezone the farmland for light industrial use, despite 200+ people attending in opposition with over 5 hours of mostly negative testimony. Opponents Samantha Scarlata and Amanda Kendall formed 'Let Sioux Falls Vote' and circulated referendum petitions, but fell short with 5,012 signatures submitted vs. the required 6,704 (5% of registered voters).

Cloverleaf Infrastructure scrapped plans in Carlton (Kewaunee County) after local residents opposed the idea. Town Chairman David Hardtke told Cloverleaf 'if this is such a good thing for the town I want the public informational meeting and they would not do that -- it's not good for the town.' Chris Kohnle of Tisch Mills Farm Center warned 'once they take land away, it'll never come back.' The developer was subsequently also rejected in the Village of Greenleaf (Brown County). Carlton's proximity to Lake Michigan made it attractive for cooling.

Microsoft purchased 240 acres in Kenosha County near I-94 and Route 142 in early 2025, with the data center scheduled to open in early 2026. The city signed an NDA with Microsoft in May 2024, keeping the project secret for six months. A resident's viral TikTok video triggered a wave of community opposition. Kenosha City Hall was standing-room only on Nov. 17 as residents packed Common Council chambers. Citizens called data centers 'parasites' and 'terrible neighbors.' A Change.org petition was created to stop the project.

Meta's $1B data center on 520 acres in Beaver Dam became controversial when it was revealed the city signed an NDA and worked with shell companies for 14 months before Meta publicly announced involvement in December 2025. The 'Beaver Dam Education on Data Center Group' is spearheading local opposition, advised by Prescott Balch, who successfully opposed Microsoft's data center in Caledonia. At least 14 residents spoke against the project at a public meeting. A lawsuit seeks release of expected energy demand from the data center. The facility is expected to open in 2027.
opposition_groups: Beaver Dam Education on Data Center Group

updates.summary: QTS Data Centers proposed a $12B project involving five data center buildings on nearly 1,600 acres of farmland in the Town of Vienna. The 'No Data Center in DeForest' group mobilized rapidly, gaining 4,000+ Facebook followers in a village of 10,000. They printed yard signs, created an online community, and voiced opposition at numerous town and village board meetings. The DeForest Village Board voted unanimously on Feb. 3, 2026 to reject the annexation. QTS formally withdrew its application.

Dutch-based Nebius plans a hyperscale data center scaling to 1.2 GW -- 18 times the electricity of all 55,000 Independence households combined. The 'Stop the AI Data Center in Independence' Facebook group, co-founded by resident Rachel Gonzalez, grew to 2,500+ members within weeks. Over 200 residents packed a February 10 meeting. Despite overwhelming opposition, the Independence City Council voted on March 2, 2026 to approve the project, issuing $150 billion in industrial revenue bonds. Nebius received a 90% personal property tax abatement and will not pay real property taxes. A Change.org petition was also created. Nebius is projected to pay $651M in PILOT fees over 20 years.
opposition_groups: Stop the AI Data Center in Independence

updates.summary: An unnamed company is seeking 400-500 acres in Cassville for a $1B data center. The 'No Data Centers in the Driftless' grassroots campaign was organized by Lemon Door restaurant owner Melodie Betts and led by Mount Hope resident Pete Moris. About 20 concerned citizens met at Lemon Door in Fennimore on Jan. 5, 2026 for an informational meeting. The group plans a webpage, Facebook page, and additional informational meetings. The Grant County Economic Development Corp. expects to learn by spring 2026 whether the proposal advances.

CyrusOne (Dallas-based) proposed a 636 MW, 1.4 million sq ft data center complex on 230 acres of farmland in Talkington Township, near Waverly, ~14 miles southwest of Springfield. Investment estimated at $500M. The zoning committee voted 5-3 against a six-month moratorium, then the ZBA recommended CyrusOne receive conditional permitted use. Union members in orange were the project's most vocal supporters. Sierra Club raised concerns about lack of transparency. The Sangamon County Board will vote March 23, 2026. County Assessor Byron Deaner estimated ~$5M/year in new property taxes.

Panattoni Development's 'Project Cannoli' is a 1 GW data center planned for property north of I-94 between Haggerty Road and I-275 on currently vacant land. Five buildings are planned. The facility would use at least 2 million gallons of water/day. The Planning Commission voted 5-2 to grant preliminary site plan approval. 1,400 people signed a petition opposing the project, citing impacts on power grid, noise, utility costs, water use, wildlife, and property values. The township board must still approve a development agreement.

Applied Digital proposed a multi-billion dollar data center campus in Deuel County near Toronto (pop. ~200), with plans for two buildings at 907,000 sq ft each offering 430 MW total IT capacity. Over 100 people attended a Jan. 6, 2026 town hall to voice concerns. A volunteer firefighter worried about emergency response capacity for a facility potentially bringing 1,000+ jobs. Residents said they'd rather pay extra property taxes than have the data center. Some held meetings during harvest season, preventing agricultural residents from attending. Applied Digital stated the project is not viable without state tax incentives.

Google confirmed it is behind a data center project on 550 acres along West State Road 42 near Monrovia. Despite resident protests, the Morgan County Plan Commission gave a favorable recommendation for rezoning. County commissioners approved additional land. Protect Morgan County group is raising funds for legal action, and residents filed a lawsuit claiming commissioners disregarded the county's comprehensive plan and that the rezoning was arbitrary. Construction began in February 2026. The Citizens Action Coalition (CAC) is also actively fighting the project.

Preserving Montgomery County LLC (formed Dec. 27, 2025 by Sabrina Cope of Truxton) filed a lawsuit in Cole County Circuit Court alleging the commission violated Missouri open meetings and open records laws (Sunshine Law violations). The suit claims the county concealed negotiations, improperly approved up to $35B in industrial revenue bonds, and gave inadequate public notice. The project, dubbed 'Project Green,' is a 1,000-acre AWS data center campus in New Florence, filed via NorthPoint Development LLC. The commission approved the tax incentive framework on Dec. 19, 2025. Scores of residents packed meetings to oppose.

A 1,300 MW data center is proposed for the sites of two former power plants in Adams County. AES Ohio filed that the data center would start at 100 MW in 2028 and ramp up to 1,300 MW (1.3 GW) by March 2032, consuming 11.4 million megawatt hours annually -- 31 times Adams County's total electricity consumption. Residents packed a community meeting in Manchester. Alex Schaffer, a data center critic running for Adams County Commission, is organizing opposition. The Economic Development Director signed NDAs about the project.
